summarization.a: The subfigure compares infrared difference spectra between 400 and 1800 cm⁻¹ recorded after BCl3→H2O and H2O→BCl3 exposures during the 13th B2O3 ALD cycle at 40 °C. Differences in the B–O stretching, B–Cl, and B–O–B bending regions show that the order of precursor exposure strongly affects surface bonding.
summarization.b: The subfigure shows corresponding difference spectra in the O–H stretching region. The presence and intensity of BO–H and hydrogen-bonded O–H features indicate different hydroxylation behavior depending on whether H2O or BCl3 is dosed first.

data_extraction.a:
| Wave number region (cm⁻¹) | Vibrational assignment | Difference between exposure sequences |
|--------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| ~1400–1600 | B–O stretch | Stronger feature for BCl3→H2O sequence |
| ~1000 | B–Cl related mode | Present after BCl3 exposure, reduced after H2O |
| ~700–800 | B–O–B bend | Appears more clearly after H2O exposure |
| ~500–600 | O–B–O bend | Small but distinct differences between sequences |
data_extraction.b:
| Wave number region (cm⁻¹) | Vibrational assignment | Difference between exposure sequences |
|--------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| ~3700 | BO–H stretch | Sharp feature associated with hydroxyl formation |
| ~3600–3200 | H-bonded O–H | Broad positive band stronger for H2O→BCl3 sequence |

a.question: How does the order of BCl3 and H2O exposure influence the infrared difference spectra?
a.answer: Changing the exposure order alters which surface bonds form first during the ALD cycle. This is reflected in differences in the B–O stretching, B–Cl, and bending modes, showing that surface chemistry depends strongly on whether chlorination or hydroxylation occurs first.

a.question: Which spectral feature indicates the presence of B–Cl related bonding?
Does the figure show that precursor sequence affects surface reactions during B2O3 ALD?

b.question: Which exposure sequence produces a stronger hydrogen-bonded O–H band?
